coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dot modified screen printed immunosensor for the determination of parathion
coredata.dc:creator: Mehta, Jyotsana
Bhardwaj, Neha
Bhardwaj, Sanjeev K.
Tuteja, Satish K.
Vinayak, Priya
Paul, A.K.
Kim, Ki-Hyun
Deep, Akash
coredata.dc:description: The widespread use of pesticides has immense effect on increased crop productions. However, they are also responsible for posing detrimental health hazards and/or for contaminating the environment with chemical residues. A routine and an on-field detection of pesticide residues in different food, water, and soil samples has become a need of the hour for which biosensors can offer a viable alternative. The present work reports a functionalized graphene quantum dot (GQD) based screen printed electrochemical immunosensor for the detection of parathion. The application of GQDs has permitted the realization of a sensitive, robust, and reproducible sensor unlike those carried out earlier for the similar purposes. This immunosensor exhibited a dynamic linear response for parathion within the range of 0.01–106 ng/L with a very low detection limit of 46 pg/L. According to the analysis of potential interferences, the proposed sensor was specifically detecting parathion even in the presence of its metabolite, paraoxon. The investigations of the proposed sensing approach with respect to stability, response reproducibility, and regeneration have fully supported its potential practical applicability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Immunosensor
Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy
Parathion
Screen-printed electrode

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) for analysis of parathion
Electrochemical impedance spectroscopic (EIS) analysis of the parathion concentrations

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic presentation of the GQDs modified screen printed immunosensor for parathion.

(A) UV–Vis spectrum of citric acid (CA), synthesized GQDs and antibody immobilized GQDs, (B) PL spectrum of GQDs at different excitation wavelengths ranging from 320 nm to 370 nm, (C) Raman spectrum of the synthesized GQDs, (D) FTIR spectra of citric acid, synthesized GQDs, amine (i.e., 2-ABA) functionalized GQDs, and anti-parathion antibody conjugated NH2GQDs.

(A) TEM image of the monodispersed GQDs, (B) AFM image and line profile of the synthesized GQDs, (C) AFM image of bare screen printed electrode, (D) AFM image of GQDs modified electrode, (E) AFM image of anti-parathion antibodies immobilized electrode.

(A) EIS measurements during different steps of immunosensor development (B) Nyquist plots depicting the variation in the R
ct values of the developed Ab/NH2GQD-SPE immunosensor as a function of varying concentrations of parathion.

(A) Calibration plot depicting R
ct values as a function of different parathion concentrations (Inset Figure shows the sensor calibration at low analyte concentrations to calculate LOD), (B) Specificity of the Ab/NH2GQD-SPE immunosensor towards the detection of 1 ng/L parathion in presence of elevated concentrations of other non-specific pesticides namely, paraoxon, malathion and chlorpyrifos, (C) Reproducibility of different GQD modified SPCE sensing electrodes for 1 ng/L concentration of parathion (Inset Figure shows the effect of incubation time between Ab/NH2GQD-SPE and 1 ng/L parathion, (D) Stability of GQD modified SPCE immunosensors response with respect to their long duration storage.
Different nanoplatforms for electrochemical sensing of different pesticides.

row.entry: Adsorption and affinity towards sulfur containing pesticides
Chelation causing MP adsorption leading to current response
Adsorption of methyl parathion resulting in current change

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: EIS-Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy; CV-Cyclic Voltammetry; DPV-Differential pulse voltammetry; SWV-Square wave voltammetry; AChE-Acetylcholinesterase; BChE-Butylcholinesterase; PTT-Poly([2,2; 5′ 2″]-terthiophene-3-carbaldehyde).

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dot modified screen printed immunosensor for the determination of parathion

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering, Hanyang University, 222 Wangsimni-Ro, Seoul 04763, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Nanoscience and Nanotechnology Lab, Central Scientific Instruments Organisation (CSIR-CSIO), Sector 30 C, Chandigarh 160030, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The widespread use of pesticides has immense effect on increased crop productions. However, they are also responsible for posing detrimental health hazards and/or for contaminating the environment with chemical residues. A routine and an on-field detection of pesticide residues in different food, water, and soil samples has become a need of the hour for which biosensors can offer a viable alternative. The present work reports a functionalized graphene quantum dot (GQD) based screen printed electrochemical immunosensor for the detection of parathion. The application of GQDs has permitted the realization of a sensitive, robust, and reproducible sensor unlike those carried out earlier for the similar purposes. This immunosensor exhibited a dynamic linear response for parathion within the range of 0.01–106 ng/L with a very low detection limit of 46 pg/L. According to the analysis of potential interferences, the proposed sensor was specifically detecting parathion even in the presence of its metabolite, paraoxon. The investigations of the proposed sensing approach with respect to stability, response reproducibility, and regeneration have fully supported its potential practical applicability.

ce:section.ce:para: A wide range of chemical compounds including carbamates, coumarin, organochlorides, organophosphates, arsenic/mercury and their derivatives, and organophosphate compounds are used most commonly to control pests in agriculture [1]. The residues of these organic pollutants are however often left to persist in all different media such as air, water, and soil. The problem becomes intensified further as the residues tend to accumulate up through the food chain [2]. Due to severe health hazard potentials, the residual pesticides can affect nervous and respiratory systems sometimes leading to fatalities [3].

To ensure the food safety and their desirable quality, different regulatory agencies monitor the level of residual pesticides (Drinking Water Guideline, 98/83/EEC, European Union, Brussels, 1998). As the indiscriminate application of harmful pesticides has been linked to several health related problems in recent years, there is a greater demand of routinely analyzing their contents in consumer products. In light of such demand, the viability of biosensors has now been well established in the quantitation of pesticide contents. The recognition components of a pesticide biosensor can be categorized into whole cells, enzymes, antibodies, nucleic acids, and aptamers [4–8]. The majorly used transducer platforms include piezoelectric, optical, and electrochemical based materials and surfaces [9–13]. Electrochemical immunosensors employing antibodies as the sensing elements offer relatively simple, label-free, specific, and miniaturized solutions [14,15]. In particular, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) based antibody immunosensors for pesticides can facilitate direct analyte detection by measuring resistance to charge transfer and diffusion across the surface [16,17]. The EIS technique measures the electrochemical response to the oscillating voltage of varying frequency while being sensitive enough to detect interfacial phenomenon [18].

The area of EIS based electrochemical immunosensing of pesticides has seen significant developments in last two decades [10,19–24]. Although the use of the screen printed electrodes has also been exploited in some studies, such systems still seek further developments [22,23,25–28]. An insight into the available information on the EIS immunosensors for pesticides reveals that the modern nanostructures (e.g., nanotubes, 2-dimensional planer sheets, quantum dots, and nanoparticles) have shown better sensor performances than other types of transduction materials in terms of sensitivity, range of detection, and limit of detection [22,24,29,30].

Among the recently developed nanostructures, the graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have attracted immense research interest for the assembly of electrochemical biosensors. GQDs can behave both as excellent electron acceptors and donors as they are structurally similar to graphene. The application areas of the GQDs include supercapacitors, sensors, photovoltaics, and fuel cells [31–38]. In the designing of nanomaterial-based sensors, the GQDs are an advantageous option with considerably low toxicity, excellent solubility, high stability, photoluminescence, easier grafting of their surface with receptors, and much desired electrical and thermal conductivities [31,36,39–42]. In particular, the presence of functional groups at their edges imparts their excellent solubility in aqueous media and suitability towards functionalization with organic, inorganic, or biological moieties [36].

The use of GQDs in the development of electrochemical sensors has been discussed in many recent works. Some of their prominent sensing applications include the systems for doxorubicin hydrochloride in human plasma, H2O2, quantification of Cr(VI) ions, etc. [43–46]. In case of GQD based immunosensors, biomolecules such as antibodies and enzymes have been immobilized and explored for the analysis of different analytes including metal ions, organic pollutants, disease markers, immunoglobulins, viruses, and toxins [38,47–51].

This present work for the first time aims to explore the use of GQDs in the development of a label-free EIS-based screen printed immunosensor for a pesticide. Note that the screen printed electrodes should offer many advantages including low sample volume, portability and cost effectiveness, low background current, wide potential range, and easy modification of the working electrode area with desired molecules [52]. The schematic of the proposed immunosensors is shown in Fig. 1 . In the process, we modified a screen printed carbon electrode with GQDs and then electrochemically modified it with 2-aminobenzyl amine to craft NH2 functionality. Subsequent incubation of the above treated electrode with the anti-parathion antibodies allowed the realization of a selective sensor system for parathion. The highlighting features of the proposed sensor include a higher sensitivity than the similar systems reported previously, wide range of analyte detection, and an inherent portability. The proposed system is demonstrated to have the potential for detecting parathion in environmental and food samples.

Most of the reagents used in the study were analytical grade purity materials purchased from Sigma/Merck. Some of the main reagents included potassium hexacyanoferrite (K3 [Fe(CN)6]), potassium hexacyanoferrate (K4 [Fe(CN)6]), 2-aminobenzyl amine (2-ABA), parathion and citric acid. The anti-parathion antibodies (Rabbit, polyclonal IgG) were purchased from Abcam. The screen printed carbon electrodes were of DropSens make and purchased from Metrohm, India. These electrodes consist of an Ag reference electrode, a Carbon counter electrode and a Carbon working electrode. The working solutions of 0.1 M of 2-ABA and 1/100th dilution of the stock anti-parathion antibodies were prepared in 1 M phosphate buffer saline (PBS, pH 7.4). The redox electrolyte probe solution was prepared by mixing 10 mM each of K3 [Fe(CN)6] and K4 [Fe(CN)6] in 0.1 M PBS. The stock solution and further dilutions of the parathion were prepared in deionized water.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL 2100 F, USA; operating at 200 kV) and atomic force microscope (AFM, XE-NSOM, Park Systems, Korea) were used to study the structural morphology/topography of the synthesized graphene quantum dots. Spectroscopic measurements were performed using Fourier transform infrared (FTIR, Nicolet iS10, USA) and Raman (Invia Renishaw, UK; Laser: 1.58 eV 785-HP-NIR) spectroscopy. Electrochemical experiments were conducted with a 600D series electrochemistry station from CH Instruments, USA. The current-voltage measurements were acquired using a two probe system from Keithley (Semiconductor Characterization System, 4200, USA).

GQDs were synthesized by the pyrolysis of citric acid (CA) as described in a previous report [53]. Briefly, 2 g of CA was first heated to 200 °C for 5 min which converted it in to a pale yellow color liquid. Continued thermal treatment for further 30 min produced an orange-color liquid. This solution was dropwise poured in to a freshly prepared 100 mL NaOH solution (10 mg/mL) maintaining stirring conditions. The reaction mixture was then neutralized to pH 7, which completed the synthesis of the water soluble GQDs.

The synthesized GQDs were drop casted onto the working area of the screen printed carbon electrodes (SPCE, working area of 12.56 mm2). The electrodes was then left to dry at 120 °C for 1 h. After ensuring the adhesion of GQDs onto electrode surface, the working electrode area (modified with the GQDs) was subjected to electropolymerization with 2-ABA. For this, 0.1 M of 2-ABA (in 1 M PBS, pH 7.4) was taken as electrolyte. The bulk electrolysis of 2-ABA was then initiated by providing a fixed potential of 0.8 V at the cathode (GQD modified working area of SPCE). During this step, the remaining counter and reference electrodes of the SPCEs were carefully masked so as to avoid any reaction on them. External Pt and Ag/AgCl electrodes were used as the counter and reference electrodes, respectively during the above bulk electrolysis reaction of 2-ABA. After 3 min of the process, the NH2 functionalized (functional groups generated as a result of bulk electrolysis of 2-ABA) GQD-SPEs was taken out and rinsed with deionized water and PBS buffer.

The immobilization of the anti-parathion antibodies on the above NH2 functionalized GQD-SPE was then achieved through stabilization of the Schiff bases between the amine functionalized GQDs and the aldehyde groups generated on the antibody as per the following procedure. A 100 μL of the diluted antibody solution was mixed with 900 μL of 0.1 M MES buffer (pH 5.5, also containing 2.1 mg/mL sodium periodate). This antibody solution was left in contact with the NH2 functionalized GQD-SPEs for 2 h at room temperature conditions (RT, 25 ± 2 °C). This prolonged incubation (2 h) of the SPEs with the antibody solution allowed the formation of the saturated bioprobe. However, when the incubation time was reduced (e.g., 15, 30, 60, and 90 min), it was unable to assure the stability of the sensor response. The sodium periodate-mediated oxidation of the carbohydrate moiety of the antibody molecules generated formyl groups which then subsequently formed a stable covalent complex with the NH2 functionalized GQD-SPEs surface. These Schiff bases were stabilized by a further incubation of the electrode with 10 μL of 5 M sodium cyanoborohydride (30 min). Next, 0.25 μg/mL of bovine serum albumin was used to block the unreacted aldehyde moieties. The above formed Ab/GQD-SPE immunosensor was finally rinsed with PBS buffer and stored at 4 °C. The masked areas (counter and reference electrodes) were also exposed by removing the masking tapes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) for analysis of parathion
ce:section.ce:para: EIS studies for the qualitative and quantitative detection of parathion were performed in a three-electrode cell using [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- as the redox couple. The impedance measurements were recorded at a fixed sinusoidal voltage of 10 mV and sweeping the frequencies between 0.1 Hz and 0.1 MHz. The frequency responses were recorded in the form of Nyquist plots which represented the imaginary impedance component (Z”, out-of-phase) against the real component (Z′, in-phase). The charge resistance transfer values were computed by fitting the impedance data with the equivalent Randles circuit. Different concentrations (10 μg/mL to 0.1 pg/mL) of the parathion analyte (5 μL) were plunged into the working area (i.e. Ab/GQD-SPE) of the sensor and left to incubate for 5–30 min. The sensor was washed with PBS buffer and then analyzed for its impedance properties towards a redox probe electrolyte. The specificity of the proposed immunosensing technique was assessed by analyzing the sensor's response towards paraoxon which is a metabolite of parathion pesticide along with other non-specific pesticides such as chlorpyrifos and malathion.

The important properties of the synthesized GQDs were investigated for different characteristics. The elemental analysis studies indicated the carbon content (w/w) in the synthesized GQDs to be 41% as compared to 35% in the base material, i.e. citric acid. This data correlates with the successful carbonization of the starting material. Upon illumination with UV light, the as-prepared yellowish brown GQDs emitted blue fluorescence (Inset of Fig. 2 A) due to the presence of abundant sp2 domains and CO functional groups similar to graphene oxide [54]. A water dispersed sample of the GQDs displayed a broad absorption peak near 340 nm (Fig. 2A), attributing to the π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains [55]. Note that the above absorption peak was not discernible in the UV spectra of citric acid. Since the GQDs should also exhibit excitation-independent photoluminescence (PL) characteristics [56], we recorded their PL spectra for different excitation wavelengths viz. 320–370 nm (Fig. 2B). The samples showed their emission wavelengths around 460 nm. The excitation wavelength-independent emission behavior can be correlated with the surface state distribution of the synthesized GQDs and the uniformity of particle size [53]. The defects trapped onto the surface along with the intrinsic quantum sized zigzag sites having triplet carbine ground state can be highlighted as prominent factors responsible for luminescence in GQDs [57].

Raman spectrum of the synthesized GQDs was collected by exciting the sample with a 514 nm laser (Fig. 2C), which helped in understanding the type and intensity of the structural defects in the synthesized GQDs. A crystalline G band around 1590 cm−1 corresponds to vibrations of sp2 rings in the two-dimensional hexagonal lattice of GQDs, whereas the disordered D band around 1350 cm−1 attributed to the scattering caused by the defects produced in sp2 hybridized termination plane of disordered carbonaceous material [58,59]. The relative ID/IG ratio of 0.85 is low enough to verify a crystalline nature and the presence of lower disorders and structural defects in the structure of the GQDs [60].

Further, FTIR spectroscopy was used to characterize the base material (i.e. CA) and synthesized GQDs (Fig. 2D). CA's FTIR spectrum shows a major band at 1650 cm−1 assignable to the CO vibration (symmetric stretching) from the COOH groups. In the sample of GQDs, significant aromatic CC bond stretching vibrations at 1635 and 1450 cm−1 suggest the aromatization of the base material to form the desired product. Multiple weak bands between 1400 and 1600 cm−1 indicate the presence of sp2 hybridized CC stretch bonding. The presence of CH bending and stretching bands at 1340 and 2970 cm−1, respectively suggest incomplete carbonization of the base material resulting in formation of GQDs. It may be noted here that the complete carbonization would have led to the synthesis of graphene oxide instead of the desired GQDs [53].

TEM (Fig. 3 A) and AFM (Fig. 3B) analysis highlighted the dispersity and particle size distribution of the synthesized GQDs within 5–15 nm. TEM imagings have illustrated a sufficient monodispersity of the synthesized nanodots. The line profile analysis of AFM data also supports the impression of the above particle diameter.

The GQD have been used for the development of an immunosensing platform for parathion. The anti-parathion antibody was immobilized onto NH2 functionalized GQD-SPE (NH2GQD-SPE). Amine functionalization on the immobilized GQDs and the subsequent bio-immobilization on the NH2GQD-SPE were confirmed by UV–Vis absorption and FTIR spectroscopy. In the UV–Vis absorption data as shown in Fig. 2A, the spectrum of the antibody bonded NH2GQD-SPE sample shows a protein specific 280 nm peak apart from the characteristics peak of the GQDs at 340 nm. This observation clearly confirms a successful conjugation of the antibodies to the GQDs surface. In FTIR spectrum (ATR mode) of the NH2GQD-SPE sample, the NH bending is suggested by the appearance of bands around 1580-1650 cm−1 while NH stretch is discernible via a band at 3250 cm−1 (Fig. 2D). In addition to this, the CN stretch is signaled around 950-1250 cm−1. These combined observations confirm a successful amine functionalization (i.e. grafting of 2-ABA) on the GQDs [61,62]. The success of the bioconjugation of NH2GQD-SPE with the anti-parathion antibodies is indicated by the presence of a weak amide bond absorption band near 1600 cm−1 (NH bend) and a broad NH stretching band around 3200 cm−1. The immobilization of the protein molecules also causes a shifting in the CO stretch from 1640 to 1690 cm−1 to the higher wavenumbers, e.g. 1670-1820 cm−1. The presence of alkene bends between 675–1000 cm−1 and C=N stretches of imine groups at 1660 cm−1 further confirms the interfacing of antibodies with the electrode.

The different stages of the sensor development have also been investigated with AFM imaging analysis (Fig. 3C–E). The bare SPE has a relatively rough surface profile (±100 nm, Fig. 3C). The modification of the SPE with GQDs improves the overall roughness of the surface to around ±20 nm (Fig. 3D). Subsequent modification of the electrode with antibodies revealed the presence of globular protein particles with the surface roughness of around ±50 nm (Fig. 3E). Thus, the AFM investigations have provided specific information during each step of the sensor development which also agrees well with the fundamental assessments. The information also highlights that the proposed sensor is fairly homogeneous with almost even distribution of the probe molecules.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical impedance spectroscopic (EIS) analysis of the parathion concentrations
ce:section.ce:para: EIS is a robust technique to analyze electrochemical immunoassays [19,20]. This technique elucidates important data on the charge transfer kinetics and uncompensated resistance of an electrode-electrolyte system. As GQDs bear a superior conduction property along with a high surface area, their bioconjugation with the specific antibodies should form the basis of an improved immunosensing platform [41]. In context to our studies with the application of an antibody conjugated NH2GQD-SPE (Ab/GQD-SPE) for the analysis of parathion, the EIS characteristics in terms of Nyquist plots have been interpreted to deduce the sensing performance of the above sensor. The equivalent electronic components of the electrochemical cell were fit in a Randle's circuit to model and compute the important values of different relevant parameters. The model of Randle's circuit takes into consideration the charge transfer resistance (Rct) at the interface of electrode/electrolyte and double layer capacitance at the electrode surface. A Nyquist curve typically represents the data in a semicircle form wherein the imaginary impedance components are plotted as a function of real impedance components. The change in the semicircle's diameter can be correlated with the changing charge transfer resistance characteristics of the sensor [63].

The EIS characteristics of the developed Ab/GQD-SPE immunosensor have been investigated at different stages of functionalization, immobilization and analyte sensing (Fig. 4 A). GQDs are efficient acceptors and transporter of electrons due to their lateral size properties [45]. They show combined characteristics of the carbon quantum dots and the graphite because of structural features with both sp2 and sp3 hybridization [47]. The above factors can be linked with a decreased surface impedance (Rct) after the modification of the carbon screen printed electrode with the GQDs (Fig. 4A). The next stage electrolytic deposition of the 2-ABA molecules on to the GQD-SPE surface leads further decrease in the (Rct) value of the electrode as the conception of amine groups on the GQDs' surface causes improvement in the charge transfer characteristics [64]. The antibody immobilization on the NH2GQD-SPE surface is found characterized with an increase in the impedance. This observation is attributed to the insulating behavior of protein molecules, which causes relative hindrance of the charge transfer across the electrolyte-electrode surface [19,22]. The EIS characterizations at different stages have confirmed the successful assembly of the desired immunosensor, which has then subsequently been explored for the analysis of parathion.

Figs. 4B and 5 A shows the results of the analysis of parathion with Ab/GQD-SPE sensor. The Nyquist plots (Fig. 4B) at different analyte concentration show a concentration proportional increment in the semicircle's diameter which is an indicative of the increase in the charge transfer resistance (Rct) values. Fig. 5A shows the range of linearity of the Rct values as a function of the quantitative analyte concentrations. The proposed sensor has offered a very wide logarithmically linear range of parathion detection, i. e 0.01–106 ng/L. The increase in the Rct values with the increasing parathion concentration can be linked with the antibody-antigen complex formation that builds up a definite kinetic barrier for the probe electrolyte molecules to diffuse through the electrode surface [19,22]. The increasing blockage of the electrode surface as a result of antibody-antigen complex formation restricts charge transfer kinetics, which is responsible for the above type of the sensor response [18].

Fig. 5A (inset) shows the sensor response vs. concentration behavior at lower sides of the investigated concentrations. This plot was used to estimate the limit of detection (LOD) of the proposed Ab/GQD-SPE sensor as per the expression: LOD = 3σ/S, where σ is the standard deviation of the sensor's response with respect to a blank analysis and S is the slope of the calibration curve (0.1406) (Fig. 5A). Thus, the estimated value of the LOD is 46 pg/L, which reflects a far better performance of the proposed sensor than previously reported antibody-based sensing systems for parathion (Table 1 ).

The specificity of the developed Ab/GQD-SPE sensor for parathion has also been investigated (Fig. 5B). The sensor is found extremely specific towards the targeted pesticide only as we did not encounter any interference from other pesticides, namely paraoxon, malathion and chlorpyrifos. Note that these specificity tests were done under elevated concentrations of the non-specific pesticides for a better assessment of the herein proposed immunosensor's utility under practical situations.

The reproducibility of the technique has been verified by studying the response of six electrodes prepared in different batches. The values of the Rct were estimated against a fixed analyte concentration for each electrode, i.e. 1 ng/L parathion. As shown in Fig. 5C, different electrodes showed insignificant variation in their response. This observation highlights that the proposed Ab/GQD-SPE sensor can be precisely assembled in order to achieve a desired level of the sensor reproducibility. The response time of the Ab/GQD-SPE sensor for the analysis of parathion was also confirmed. For this, the values of the Rct were recorded with respect to the varying incubation times. The results of these studies (Fig. 5C inset) highlighted that a minimum of 15 min incubation time was required to achieve a stable sensor reading. In all other measurements, we used an incubation time of 15 min before the EIS analysis.

The stability of Ab/GQD-SPE sensors against prolonged storage conditions has also been assessed, for which several sensors were stored under refrigerated conditions (4 °C). These electrodes were tested for their response against 1 ng/L parathion during periodic intervals. As shown in Fig. 5D, the sensor response was retained significantly constant up to 60 days of the storage.

The regeneration of the used sensor is important from a commercial point of view. Therefore, we have investigated the possibility of the regeneration of the herein proposed Ab/GQD-SPE sensor. After an analysis of 1 ng/L of parathion, the sensor was washed subsequently with 10 mM Glycine-HCl solution and PBS buffer. It was reused to study any variation in the response. It was observed, the Ab/GQD-SPE sensor could retain almost 80% of its initial activity even after 5 regeneration cycles. This important observation speaks about the viability of the proposed Ab/GQD-SPE sensor for the effective and efficient analysis of parathion.

In conclusion, we have investigated an amine functionalized GQD based SPE immunosensors for highly sensitive detection of parathion. The application of GQDs, in comparison to the graphene sheets, seems to have offered certain advantages. The GQDs are easily decorated with more homogeneity on the screen printed surface which leads to more efficient control on sensor precision and accuracy. Unlike the graphene sheets, the GQDs can be obtained with a relatively uniform size distribution. In-situ availability of functional groups on the GQD surface helps in a robust assembly of 2-aminobenzylamine moieties to facilitate the amine functionality which has been used to decorate anti-parathion antibodies on the sensor. As highlighted in Table 1, the herein proposed technique has offered a lower limit of detection and broader range of parathion analysis than most of the recently reported similar studies, In the present study, GQDs have been explored as substrate for immobilization of antibodies to facilitate immunosensing of parathion. The GQDs may act both as the electron acceptor and electron donor. This effect accounts to their efficient electrocatalytic performance such as better sensitivity than other graphene-based immunosensors. Compared to many other electrochemical substrates, e.g. noble metals, metal compounds, CNTs, and polymers, the biocompatible GQDs are found to have improved properties in solubility and stability. The performance of the proposed sensor, when compared in terms of important sensor parameters with previously reported pesticide sensors (including graphene sheets based immunosensors developed previously by our study group), clearly showed many advantages. Most importantly, the selection of GQDs as an electrochemical substrate for the sensing of parathion offered the best detectability with a highly dynamic range of analysis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: JM is grateful to University Grant Commission, India for her research fellowship. The financial support from CSIR, India (Project OMEGA/PSC0202/2.2.5) is thankfully acknowledged. The authors also thank Director, CSIR CSIO, Chandigarh for providing all the infrastructural facilities. KHK acknowledges support made in part by grants from the National Research Foundation of Korea funded by the Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning (No. 2016R1E1A1A01940995).
